deepseek-r1-distill-llama-8b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this patient.
Let me go through the case step by step.
The patient is a 27-year-old woman presenting with a pruritic lesion in her left ear canal that's been recurrent, with bleeding.
She had a similar issue a year ago, treated with some topical medication which helped, but then it recurred.
She was seen by another doctor who prescribed some antibiotics and a steroid ointment, but that didn't work.
Now she's referred to this clinic.
Her past history is clean in terms of otitis media, head trauma, or noise exposure.
She doesn't smoke or drink, and no allergies.
She works in a health field, so maybe some exposure to infections, but not sure if that's relevant.
On exam, there's a raised lesion on the posterior tragus of the left ear, cobblestone appearance with vessels.
Under the microscope, it extends into the anterior wall of the external auditory canal.
No tragal tenderness.
Rinne test is positive bilaterally, meaning air conduction is better than bone, so probably normal hearing.
Weber test shows sound in the midline, so no lateralization.
The culture grew coagulase-negative staphylococcus.
They treated with nystatin and triamcinolone, but it didn't help.
Then she comes back with increased pruritus and bleeding, a small crust, and they do a diagnostic procedure.
Hmm, coagulase-negative staphylococcus is often part of the normal flora, but when it's causing infection, it's usually in the presence of an underlying condition.
The lesion's description sounds like a granuloma, maybe.
